params.text:
You should not suffer for speaking up

PIDA makes it unlawful for a worker to be treated badly because they have made a protected disclosure. This treatment is called detriment. It could include being dismissed, demoted, denied training or shifts, bullied, or pushed out. If you are dismissed because of a protected disclosure, that dismissal is automatically unfair, and an employment tribunal can order compensation or reinstatement.

You are protected when you raise a concern honestly and reasonably. Since 2013 you no longer have to prove you acted in “good faith” to be protected — but a tribunal may reduce any award if a disclosure was not made in good faith. In practice, this means: speak up because you genuinely believe something is wrong, not to cause mischief, and you are on solid ground.

params.alt: Raising a concern with a manager

params.question: A care worker who is dismissed because they made a protected disclosure has been automatically unfairly dismissed.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Under PIDA, dismissing a worker because of a protected disclosure is automatically unfair.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Dismissal for making a protected disclosure is automatically unfair under PIDA, whatever the worker's length of service.
